Question: A woman in her late 20s presented with blurry vision in both eyes of 2-week duration. Her ocular history included optic neuropathy 5 years prior to presentation. Her medical history included acute lymphocytic leukemia (currently in remission) after an allogenic hematopoetic stem cell transplant. Two months before presentation, she developed Bell palsy on the right side of her face for which she was being treated with oral prednisone; her other medications were oral contraceptives and multivitamins. As her Bell palsy was improving, she noted worsening of her visual acuity. Her best-corrected visual acuity was hand motion OD and 20/400 OS. Tensions were 12 mm Hg bilaterally. A slitlamp examination revealed normal anterior segments in each eye. The vitreous was free of cells in each eye. A dilated ophthalmoscopic examination revealed mild optic nerve pallor (associated with her past episodes of optic neuritis) (Figure). Fluorescein angiography demonstrated no leakage.Ask the patient to taper the oral prednisone and reassure her that the symptoms will gradually resolveAdminister intravitreal injection of an anti vascular endothelial growth factor agent What Would You Do Next?
Options: A:Ask the patient to taper the oral prednisone and reassure her that the symptoms will gradually resolve B:Administer intravitreal injection of an anti vascular endothelial growth factor agent C:Order brain neuroimaging and lumbar puncture D:Order an infectious and inflammatory workup
 Which option is the correct answer to the question? ALWAYS provide the option index of the final answer AT THE END with TRIPLE BACKTICKS among A, B, C, D, EXACTLY like this: The answer is ```X```. DO NOT add any other text or variations.
caption: Color fundus photograph of the right eye showing a pale nerve and mildly dilated and tortuous veins.
opa: Ask the patient to taper the oral prednisone and reassure her that the symptoms will gradually resolve
opb: Administer intravitreal injection of an anti vascular endothelial growth factor agent
opc: Order brain neuroimaging and lumbar puncture
opd: Order an infectious and inflammatory workup
diagnosis: Serous macular detachment secondary to central nervous system involvement by acute lymphoblastic leukemia
answer: Order brain neuroimaging and lumbar puncture
explanation: This is a case of a young woman with bilateral serous macular detachment. The differential diagnosis includes Vogt-Koyanagi-Harada syndrome, central serous chorioretinopathy, uveal effusion syndrome, hematologic malignant tumors, paraproteinemias, choroidal hemangioma, choroidal metastasis, malignant hypertension, and toxemia of pregnancy. Her complete blood cell count was normal. Magnetic resonance imaging of her brain revealed a marked hyperintense fluid-attenuated inversion recovery signal within the sulci of the cerebral hemispheres bilaterally with leptomeningeal enhancement. Cerebrospinal fluid analysis showed 400 white blood cells per cubic millimeter, and the opening pressure was 50 cm H2O. Cytologic analysis of the cerebrospinal fluid was positive for blasts. A bone marrow biopsy revealed no relapse of acute lymphocytic leukemia and 100% donor chimerism. A liver biopsy disclosed no graft vs host disease. The diagnosis that was made was bilateral serous retinopathy due to a relapse of acute lymphocytic leukemia of the central nervous system.Central serous retinopathy is a very common cause of serous macular detachment. However, the absence of a focal leakage point on the fluorescein angiogram made it unlikely. Therefore, observation and counseling against corticosteroids would not be advisable. Anti vascular endothelial growth factor treatment would not be the next best step because a bilateral serous retinal detachment without angiographic leakage points is unlikely to be driven by vascular endothelial growth factor. Inflammatory and infectious workup is less likely to yield an answer in a case with a paucity of inflammatory symptoms and signs in both eyes. Vogt-Koyanagi-Harada syndrome can be associated with serous retinal detachment and neurologic findings. Vogt-Koyanagi-Harada syndrome was less plausible in our case given the absence of some cardinal features of the disease, such as inflammatory cells in the anterior chamber and/or the vitreous, auditory signs, and dermal findings such as vitiligo and poliosis.1 Given the patient s history of acute lymphocytic leukemia and the presence of bilateral serous macular detachment, the probability of a relapse of acute lymphocytic leukemia was very high. Therefore, the next best step would be to have the patient undergo neuroimaging and a lumbar puncture.Leukemic retinopathy can present with nerve fiber layer infarcts, white-centered hemorrhages, subhyaloid or subinternal limiting membrane hemorrhages, infiltrates of the retina and/or of the optic nerve head, choroidal infiltration, serous retinal detachment, and vascular occlusions. The most reported ocular finding in leukemia is retinal vascular changes.2 Serous retinal detachment is a less common manifestation of ocular leukemia.3 The pathophysiologic mechanism behind the serous retinal detachment is not completely understood. The predominant theory describes the infiltration of leukemic cells into the choroidal capillaries, leading to partial vascular occlusion and subsequent circulatory disturbances.4 Secondary dysfunction of the retinal pigment epithelium and the Bruch membrane may ultimately lead to serous retinal detachment over the macular region.4 Leukemic cell infiltration has been demonstrated in cadaveric ocular histologic samples and is believed to contribute to choroidal ischemia and subsequent retinal pigment epithelium dysfunction.5Clinicians should maintain a high level of suspicion for leukemia when encountering patients with a bilateral serous retinal detachment. In some cases, the retinal detachment may be the presenting manifestation.6-8 Among the differential diagnoses of serous retinal detachment, leukemia is a life-threatening condition and should be diagnosed and treated promptly.The patient was treated with intrathecal combination chemotherapy consisting of cytarabine, hydrocortisone acetate, and methotrexate sodium. After 6 doses of intrathecal chemotherapy, her neurologic symptoms disappeared, the serous macular detachment resolved, and her visual acuity returned to her baseline values (20/200 OD and 20/20 OS).
